name: Ivory Coast
tagline: From the Dyula trading empire of Kong and the Baoulé migration under Queen Abla Pokou to French conquest, the cocoa-fuelled 'Ivorian miracle' under Houphouët-Boigny, and the civil wars that tested the nation.
summary: Côte d'Ivoire takes its European name from the ivory once shipped from its coast. Long before that trade, its northern savannas were drawn into the trans-Saharan commerce of the Ghana and Mali empires, and Mandé merchants known as the Dyula built the trading city of Kong into the heart of an empire founded about 1710 that grew rich on gold and kola nuts. In the mid-eighteenth century the Baoulé arrived from the east, led by tradition under Queen Abla Pokou, who broke from the Asante; other Akan refugees founded the Agni kingdoms, while Senufo and Mandé peoples held the north. France made the territory a colony in 1893, crushing the resistance of Samori Touré by 1898, and bound it into French West Africa as a forced-labour economy producing cocoa and coffee. The planter Félix Houphouët-Boigny led the campaign that abolished forced labour in 1946 and brought the country to independence on 7 August 1960. Decades of one-party stability and rapid growth — the 'Ivorian miracle' — gave way after his death in 1993 to disputes over the doctrine of 'Ivoirité', a civil war from 2002, and a violent post-election crisis in 2010–2011, before a fragile recovery under Alassane Ouattara.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: On 7 August 1960 Côte d'Ivoire became a fully independent republic, ending more than six decades of French colonial rule and a brief period as an autonomous republic within the French Community. Its first president was Félix Houphouët-Boigny, a wealthy Baoulé planter and physician who had risen through French colonial politics, founded the African Agricultural Union (Syndicat Agricole Africain), and helped abolish forced labour in the French Empire in 1946. Unlike many of his contemporaries, Houphouët-Boigny had favoured a continued close association with France, and he kept Abidjan firmly within the French orbit after independence. The new state was already the most prosperous in French West Africa, its economy built on cocoa and coffee, and it entered nationhood with the relative stability that would underpin the so-called 'Ivorian miracle' of the following two decades. Houphouët-Boigny would rule, unopposed for most of that time, until his death in 1993.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From France on 7 August 1960, under Félix Houphouët-Boigny

quickFacts.label: Name
quickFacts.value: From the ivory trade of its coast; the country insists on the French form Côte d'Ivoire

quickFacts.label: Kong Empire
quickFacts.value: Dyula (Mandé) trading state founded about 1710 by Seku Watara, rich on gold and kola

quickFacts.label: Baoulé migration
quickFacts.value: Akan migrants arrived in the mid-1700s, led by tradition under Queen Abla Pokou from the Asante

quickFacts.label: Colonial economy
quickFacts.value: Forced-labour cocoa and coffee cultivation within French West Africa, 1893–1960

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Political capital moved to Yamoussoukro in 1983; Abidjan remains the economic centre

eras.title: The northern savannas, the Dyula, and the Empire of Kong
eras.standfirst: Drawn into the trans-Saharan trade of the great Sahelian empires, the northern savannas became home to Senufo farmers and to Mandé merchants — the Dyula — whose town of Kong grew into the seat of an empire trading gold and kola.
body: The lands that became Côte d'Ivoire were settled over many centuries by more than sixty peoples. In the wooded savannas of the north lived the Senufo, farmers whose chief centre was the trading town of Korhogo, and the Northern Mandé, including the Malinké. From the eleventh century onward, as the rulers of the Ghana and then the Mali empires embraced Islam and pushed their commerce southward, these northern peoples were drawn into the trans-Saharan trade networks. Kola nuts gathered at the edge of the southern forest were among the prized goods carried north, exchanged for salt, cloth, and the gold that flowed across the desert to North Africa and the Mediterranean.

The carriers of this commerce were Mandé-speaking Muslim merchants known as the Dyula (or Juula), who fanned out from the Mali heartland to found trading and Islamic centres across the region. By tradition they established towns such as Kong, Bondoukou, Bouna, and Begho on the northern fringe of the forest, where the products of the rainforest met the routes of the Sahel. These merchant communities formed a literate, Muslim commercial class threaded through a countryside of non-Muslim cultivators, a pattern that would shape the north for centuries.

Early in the eighteenth century the Dyula of Kong were united by Seku Watara, a Mandé warrior and merchant who claimed descent from the Keita lineage of Mali. About 1710 he founded the state often called the Kong or Wattara Empire, centred on Kong in the north-east of modern Côte d'Ivoire and reaching into present-day Burkina Faso, Mali, and Ghana. With firm control of the crucial trade routes, Kong became a flourishing centre of commerce in both gold and kola nuts, and a notable seat of Islamic learning, for nearly two centuries.

events.title: The savannas join the trans-Saharan trade
events.summary: As the Ghana and Mali empires expanded southward, the northern peoples of the region — Senufo and Mandé — were drawn into trans-Saharan commerce, exchanging forest kola nuts for salt, cloth, and gold.

events.title: Dyula merchants found northern trading towns
events.summary: Mandé-speaking Muslim merchants, the Dyula, established trading and Islamic centres such as Kong, Bondoukou, Bouna, and Begho, linking the rainforest to the markets of the Sahel.

events.title: Seku Watara founds the Empire of Kong
events.summary: Seku Watara united the Dyula of Kong into a state, often called the Wattara Empire, that grew rich controlling the gold and kola trade and reached into present-day Burkina Faso, Mali, and Ghana.

events.title: Kong expands northward
events.summary: Under Seku Watara and his kin the empire of Kong extended its reach, conquering toward Bobo-Dioulasso and cementing its dominance over the regional trade routes before central authority weakened after his death about 1745.

pullquote.text: Kong became a centre of trade for both gold and kola nuts — a Muslim commercial city threaded into a countryside of non-Muslim cultivators.

eras.title: The Akan migrations: Baoulé and Agni kingdoms
eras.standfirst: In the mid-eighteenth century waves of Akan peoples fled the rising Asante Empire to the east, founding the Baoulé kingdom under the legendary Queen Abla Pokou and the Agni kingdoms of Indénié and Sanwi.
body: While the Dyula built their commercial empire in the north, the east-central region was transformed by migration. The Akan peoples of the forest, whose great state of Asante was coalescing in present-day Ghana around 1700, sent offshoots westward into what is now Côte d'Ivoire. Some came as conquerors and traders; others were refugees from the dynastic struggles of the Asante. By the mid-eighteenth century these movements had planted a cluster of Akan states across the south-east of the territory.

The most celebrated was the Baoulé. Oral tradition holds that, in the years around 1750, a royal woman remembered as Abla Pokou — a niece or kinswoman of the Asante royal house — broke away from the Asante after a succession dispute and led her followers west. According to the legend, halted at the swollen Comoé River, Pokou was told that only the sacrifice of a noble child would let her people cross; she gave up her only son to the waters, and as she mourned she cried 'Ba-ouli' — 'the child is dead' — a phrase said to have given the Baoulé their name. The Baoulé settled around Sakasso and became, in time, the largest single people of the country.

Other Akan refugees fleeing the Asante founded the Agni (Anyi) kingdoms of Indénié and Sanwi in the south-east, near the lagoons and the Gold Coast frontier. These forest kingdoms, with their gold, their stools, and their Akan institutions, gave the south-east a political character distinct from the Mandé and Senufo north — a lasting division between the Akan-influenced forest and the savanna that would echo through the country's later history. The legacy ran deep: the first president, Houphouët-Boigny, was himself a Baoulé.

events.title: Akan states form on the eastern frontier
events.summary: As the Asante Empire rose in present-day Ghana, Akan groups such as the Abron founded states near the eastern edge of modern Côte d'Ivoire, beginning a long era of Akan migration into the region.

events.title: The Baoulé migrate under Queen Abla Pokou
events.summary: By tradition, Queen Abla Pokou broke from the Asante after a succession struggle and led her people west across the Comoé River, founding the Baoulé kingdom around Sakasso — the largest people of modern Côte d'Ivoire.

events.title: The Agni kingdoms of Indénié and Sanwi
events.summary: Other Akan groups fleeing the Asante established the Agni (Anyi) kingdoms of Indénié and Sanwi in the south-east, near the lagoons and the Gold Coast, extending Akan institutions across the forest zone.

figures.name: Abla Pokou
figures.role: Queen and founder of the Baoulé
figures.blurb: By tradition a kinswoman of the Asante royal house, Pokou led her followers west into modern Côte d'Ivoire after a succession dispute. The legend that she sacrificed her only son to cross the Comoé River — crying 'Ba-ouli', 'the child is dead' — gives the Baoulé their name.

pullquote.text: She cried 'Ba-ouli' — 'the child is dead' — and gave a people its name.
pullquote.attribution: Baoulé tradition of Queen Abla Pokou

eras.title: The ivory coast, the French, and the conquest
eras.standfirst: European traders worked the coast for ivory and other goods from the fifteenth century; in the nineteenth, France turned trade into protectorates and a colony, declared in 1893 and secured by the defeat of Samori Touré in 1898.
body: European ships had touched this shore since the late fifteenth century — the first recorded French voyage to the West African coast dates to 1483 — and traders gave its sections names drawn from their cargoes: the Grain Coast, the Gold Coast, and the Ivory Coast, the last for the elephant tusks shipped from its lagoons. Unlike neighbouring shores, the coast had few natural harbours and a dangerous surf, so for centuries European contact was limited and intermittent. The French established a short-lived mission at Assinie in 1687, and in the 1840s the naval officer Bouët-Willaumez signed treaties with coastal rulers around Grand-Bassam and Assinie, giving France toeholds on the lagoon coast.

The wider scramble for Africa transformed these footholds into a colony. From 1887 the explorer Louis-Gustave Binger journeyed through the interior, and he and the trader Marcel Treich-Laplène negotiated a web of protectorate treaties with local rulers. Britain recognised French claims, and on 10 March 1893 France formally constituted the colony of Côte d'Ivoire, with Binger as its first governor. Its boundaries were fixed by agreements with Liberia and Britain over the following years.

Establishing real control took far longer than declaring the colony. In the savanna the empire-builder Samori Touré, who from 1878 had forged a vast Mandé state across present-day Guinea, Mali, and northern Côte d'Ivoire, resisted the French for years, employing scorched-earth tactics and sacking Kong in the late 1890s. French columns finally captured Samori on 29 September 1898 at Guélémou and exiled him to Gabon, where he died in 1900. With his fall, organised resistance in the north collapsed, though pacification of the forest peoples continued violently into the 1910s.

events.title: Earliest recorded French voyage to the coast
events.summary: French mariners reached the West African coast; over the following centuries European traders worked the shore for ivory, gold, and other goods, giving the Ivory Coast its lasting name, though its surf-bound coast limited permanent contact.

events.title: France signs treaties on the lagoon coast
events.summary: The naval officer Bouët-Willaumez concluded treaties with rulers around Grand-Bassam and Assinie, securing the first durable French trading footholds on the coast of present-day Côte d'Ivoire.

events.title: France constitutes the colony of Côte d'Ivoire
events.summary: After protectorate treaties negotiated by Binger and Treich-Laplène, France formally established the colony of Côte d'Ivoire on 10 March 1893, naming Louis-Gustave Binger its first governor.

events.title: Capture of Samori Touré ends northern resistance
events.summary: The Mandé empire-builder Samori Touré, who had resisted the French for years and sacked Kong, was captured at Guélémou on 29 September 1898 and exiled to Gabon, breaking organised resistance in the savanna.

figures.name: Samori Touré
figures.blurb: From 1878 Samori forged a powerful Muslim empire across present-day Guinea, Mali, and northern Côte d'Ivoire, and resisted French conquest for more than a decade with scorched-earth warfare. Captured in 1898 and exiled to Gabon, he became an enduring symbol of African resistance to colonialism.

pullquote.text: Declaring the colony in 1893 was the easy part; it took until 1898, and the fall of Samori Touré, to begin to make French rule real.

eras.title: Colony, forced labour, and the road to independence
eras.standfirst: Within French West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire became a cash-crop colony built on forced labour and head taxes — until the planter Félix Houphouët-Boigny led the campaign that abolished forced labour in 1946 and brought the colony to independence in 1960.
body: As a constituent territory of the Federation of French West Africa from 1904, Côte d'Ivoire was governed for the profit of the metropole. France imposed a head tax in 1900, provoking revolts, and a system of forced labour that compelled African men to work without pay — building roads and railways and, above all, labouring on the cocoa, coffee, and timber plantations that French and African planters carved out of the forest. Recruitment of labourers, often dragooned from the densely populated lands later organised as Upper Volta, fed an export economy that made the colony France's richest in West Africa but rested on coercion and hardship.

Out of the planter class came the colony's defining political figure. Félix Houphouët-Boigny, born about 1905 into a Baoulé chiefly family near Yamoussoukro and trained as a physician, organised African cocoa and coffee growers — who were denied the privileges granted to European planters — into the African Agricultural Union (Syndicat Agricole Africain) in 1944. Elected to the French Constituent Assembly, he won his greatest early victory in 1946 when the French parliament abolished forced labour throughout the empire, a reform indelibly associated with his name. The same year he helped found the inter-territorial African Democratic Rally (RDA) and its Ivorian branch, the Democratic Party of Côte d'Ivoire (PDCI).

Post-war reforms steadily widened African political rights, and Houphouët-Boigny, having served as a minister in French governments in Paris, steered his country toward self-rule by negotiation rather than rupture. In a 1958 referendum Côte d'Ivoire chose to become an autonomous republic within the French Community. Full independence followed on 7 August 1960, with Houphouët-Boigny as the new republic's first president — a leader who, almost uniquely among African nationalists, sought to preserve close ties with the former colonial power.

events.title: Côte d'Ivoire joins French West Africa
events.summary: The colony became a constituent territory of the Federation of French West Africa, governed from Dakar, and was developed as a source of cocoa, coffee, and timber for the metropole.

events.title: Head tax and forced labour
events.summary: France imposed a head tax in 1900 and a forced-labour regime that compelled African men to work without pay on infrastructure and plantations, drawing recruits from the lands later organised as Upper Volta and provoking repeated revolts.

events.title: Houphouët-Boigny founds the African Agricultural Union
events.summary: Félix Houphouët-Boigny organised African cocoa and coffee planters into the Syndicat Agricole Africain to win them the rights enjoyed by European growers — the start of his rise to political leadership.

events.title: Forced labour abolished
events.summary: On a bill closely associated with Houphouët-Boigny, the French parliament abolished forced labour throughout the empire in 1946; the same year he helped found the inter-territorial RDA and its Ivorian party, the PDCI.

events.title: Independence under Houphouët-Boigny
events.summary: After choosing autonomy within the French Community in a 1958 referendum, Côte d'Ivoire became a fully independent republic on 7 August 1960, with Félix Houphouët-Boigny as its first president.

pullquote.text: The colony was France's richest in West Africa — and its wealth was built on a forced-labour cash-crop economy.

eras.title: The Ivorian miracle and the long Houphouët-Boigny era
eras.standfirst: For three decades Houphouët-Boigny ruled a one-party state whose cocoa-and-coffee boom made it one of Africa's most prosperous nations — a stability crowned, and strained, by grand projects such as the vast basilica at Yamoussoukro.
body: Independent Côte d'Ivoire enjoyed a period of rapid growth so striking it was dubbed the 'Ivorian miracle'. Pursuing a liberal, export-oriented economy in close partnership with France and welcoming foreign capital and migrant labour, the country became one of the world's largest producers of cocoa and coffee. By the early 1980s it had one of the highest per-capita incomes in sub-Saharan Africa among states without oil, and Abidjan grew into a glittering commercial metropolis. Houphouët-Boigny used the proceeds to bind the nation together through public employment and patronage, while ruling through a single party, the PDCI, with himself unchallenged at its head.

The model had limits. The boom depended on world commodity prices, and when cocoa and coffee prices collapsed in the early 1980s the miracle faltered, leaving heavy debts and growing discontent. The economy's appetite for labour had also drawn millions of migrants from neighbouring countries, especially Burkina Faso, settling questions of belonging and citizenship that would later turn poisonous. In 1983 the president made his home village of Yamoussoukro the official political capital, and there he built the Basilica of Our Lady of Peace — modelled on St Peter's in Rome and recognised as the largest church in the world — consecrated by Pope John Paul II in 1990 amid controversy over its enormous cost.

Under domestic and international pressure, Houphouët-Boigny legalised opposition parties and held the country's first multiparty presidential election in 1990, which he won comfortably against the historian Laurent Gbagbo. But the 'Old Man', as he was known, was ageing, and he had named no clear successor. When he died in office on 7 December 1993, after more than three decades in power, he left behind both Africa's most famous post-colonial success story and an unresolved question of who, and what, would come after him.

events.title: The 'Ivorian miracle'
events.summary: Liberal, export-led growth built on cocoa and coffee and close ties to France made Côte d'Ivoire one of the most prosperous states in sub-Saharan Africa, with Abidjan a thriving commercial hub.

events.title: Commodity slump ends the boom
events.summary: A collapse in world cocoa and coffee prices ended the era of rapid growth, leaving heavy debt and rising discontent that would strain the one-party system.

events.title: Capital moved to Yamoussoukro
events.summary: Houphouët-Boigny designated his birthplace, Yamoussoukro, the official political capital, though Abidjan remained the economic and administrative centre.

events.title: Basilica of Our Lady of Peace consecrated
events.summary: The vast basilica at Yamoussoukro, modelled on St Peter's in Rome and recognised as the largest church in the world, was consecrated by Pope John Paul II amid controversy over its enormous cost.

events.title: First multiparty election
events.summary: Under domestic and international pressure Houphouët-Boigny legalised opposition parties and won the country's first multiparty presidential election against the historian Laurent Gbagbo.

events.title: Death of Houphouët-Boigny
events.summary: The country's founding president died in office after more than three decades in power, leaving a celebrated record of stability and growth but no clearly prepared successor.

figures.name: Félix Houphouët-Boigny
figures.blurb: A Baoulé planter and physician who organised African growers, helped abolish forced labour in 1946, and led the country to independence in 1960. He ruled for over three decades, presiding over the 'Ivorian miracle' and building the basilica at Yamoussoukro, while keeping close ties to France.

pullquote.text: By the early 1980s it had one of the highest incomes in sub-Saharan Africa among states without oil — the so-called Ivorian miracle.

eras.title: Ivoirité, civil war, and recovery
eras.standfirst: The succession to Houphouët-Boigny unleashed the divisive politics of 'Ivoirité', a 1999 coup, civil war from 2002, and a violent post-election crisis in 2010–2011 — before a fragile recovery under Alassane Ouattara.
body: Houphouët-Boigny was succeeded by Henri Konan Bédié, the head of parliament, who consolidated power partly by promoting the doctrine of 'Ivoirité' — a notion of authentic Ivorian belonging that questioned the citizenship of northerners and of the descendants of immigrant labourers. The chief target was Alassane Ouattara, a northern Muslim and former prime minister, who was barred from elections on contested grounds about his parentage. The doctrine inflamed the old division between the largely Christian and animist south and the Muslim north, and corroded national unity. On 24 December 1999 Bédié was overthrown in the country's first coup d'état, led by General Robert Guéï.

Disputed elections in 2000 brought Laurent Gbagbo to the presidency after Guéï tried to claim victory and was forced out by street protests. Tensions snapped on 19 September 2002, when an army mutiny grew into a rebellion that split the country between a government-held south and a rebel-held north under the Forces Nouvelles. French and United Nations peacekeepers separated the two sides, and after years of stalemate the Ouagadougou Political Agreement of 2007 brought the rebel leader Guillaume Soro into government and pointed toward elections.

The long-delayed election of 2010 produced catastrophe. The electoral commission declared Ouattara the winner of the runoff, but Gbagbo, backed by the Constitutional Council, refused to concede; both men claimed the presidency and took the oath. The standoff escalated into open conflict in early 2011, killing some 3,000 people, until pro-Ouattara forces, supported by French and UN troops, captured Gbagbo in Abidjan on 11 April 2011. Ouattara took office and oversaw a return to rapid economic growth, though the wounds of 'Ivoirité' and the wars healed slowly; he was re-elected in 2015 and won a contested third term in 2020 that his opponents called unconstitutional.

events.title: The politics of 'Ivoirité'
events.summary: President Bédié promoted 'Ivoirité', a doctrine of authentic Ivorian belonging used to exclude rivals such as Alassane Ouattara and to question the citizenship of northerners and immigrants, deepening a north–south rift.

events.title: The first coup d'état
events.summary: General Robert Guéï overthrew President Henri Konan Bédié in the country's first military coup, ending the PDCI's unbroken hold on power since independence.

events.title: Civil war divides the country
events.summary: An army mutiny grew into a rebellion that split Côte d'Ivoire between a government-held south and a rebel-held north under the Forces Nouvelles, drawing in French and UN peacekeepers.

events.title: Ouagadougou Political Agreement
events.summary: A peace accord brokered through regional mediation brought rebel leader Guillaume Soro into a unity government and set a path toward reunification and elections, easing the first civil war.

events.title: Post-election crisis
events.summary: After the disputed November 2010 runoff, Gbagbo refused to cede to Ouattara; the standoff became open conflict that killed about 3,000 people before pro-Ouattara forces, backed by France and the UN, captured Gbagbo on 11 April 2011.

events.title: Recovery under Ouattara
events.summary: Alassane Ouattara took office and presided over a return to rapid growth; he was re-elected in 2015 and won a contested third term in 2020 that opponents argued was unconstitutional.

figures.name: Laurent Gbagbo
figures.blurb: A historian and long-time opposition leader who became president in 2000. His refusal to concede the 2010 election to Ouattara triggered the post-election crisis; captured in 2011, he was later tried and acquitted at the International Criminal Court.

figures.name: Alassane Ouattara
figures.blurb: A northern Muslim economist and former prime minister long excluded from power by the politics of 'Ivoirité'. Recognised as winner of the 2010 election, he took office after Gbagbo's capture in 2011 and oversaw economic recovery, winning further terms in 2015 and 2020.

pullquote.text: Two men claimed the presidency and took the oath; the standoff cost some 3,000 lives before it broke.

figures.name: Félix Houphouët-Boigny
figures.blurb: Baoulé planter, physician, and architect of independence who abolished forced labour in 1946, led the country to independence in 1960, and ruled for over three decades during the 'Ivorian miracle'.

figures.name: Abla Pokou
figures.role: Queen and founder of the Baoulé (c. 18th century)
figures.blurb: Legendary queen who led the Baoulé west from the Asante in the mid-1700s; the tale of sacrificing her son to cross the Comoé River gives her people their name.

figures.name: Samori Touré
figures.blurb: Founder of a vast West African empire who resisted the French for over a decade before his capture in 1898 — an enduring symbol of African resistance to colonial conquest.
